evals.name: error-handling-library
evals.prompt: I'm writing a database library crate. Create an error type for connection failures, query errors, and timeout errors. Use anyhow for the error handling.
evals.expected_output: Should use thiserror (not anyhow) for library crates, with #[from] for automatic conversions.

assertions.text: Uses thiserror for library error types
Does not use anyhow for library error enum
assertions.description: Should explain that libraries use thiserror, applications use anyhow, and define a proper error enum

assertions.text: Defines error enum with derive(Error, Debug)
Uses #[from] for automatic From conversions

evals.name: mutex-across-await
evals.prompt: I have an Arc<Mutex<Vec<String>>> shared between async tasks. Write an async function that locks the mutex, pushes a value, then calls an async API. The compiler says the future is not Send.
evals.expected_output: Should scope the MutexGuard in a block before awaiting, or suggest tokio::sync::Mutex.

assertions.text: Drops MutexGuard before await point
assertions.description: Should scope the lock in a block that ends before the .await, or use tokio::sync::Mutex

assertions.text: Explains the Send bound requirement for tokio::spawn
assertions.description: Should explain that MutexGuard is !Send and futures must be Send for tokio::spawn

assertions.text: Does not hold std::sync::MutexGuard across await

evals.name: cancellation-safety-select
evals.prompt: Write a loop that reads lines from a TCP stream and also listens for a shutdown signal using tokio::select!. Use AsyncBufReadExt::read_line for reading.
evals.expected_output: Should warn about cancellation safety of read_line and use a safe pattern.

assertions.text: Warns about read_line being cancel-unsafe
assertions.description: Should explain that read_line is not cancel-safe in select! and partial data can be lost

assertions.text: Uses a cancel-safe pattern
assertions.description: Should use tokio::spawn wrapper, pin the future, or suggest cancel-safe alternatives

assertions.text: Uses select! macro

evals.name: typestate-builder
evals.prompt: Create a request builder in Rust where url and method are required but headers and body are optional. Make sure it's impossible to call build() without setting url and method. Use regular Rust types, no validation at runtime.
evals.expected_output: Should use typestate pattern with PhantomData to enforce required fields at compile time.

assertions.text: Uses PhantomData for state tracking
build() only available when required fields are set
assertions.description: build() should be on an impl block constrained to the state where both url and method have been provided

assertions.text: No runtime validation for required fields
Consumes self to transition states
assertions.description: Methods should take self (not &self) to consume the old state and return a new state

evals.name: trait-object-vs-generic
evals.prompt: I have a plugin system where users can register handlers at runtime. Each handler processes events differently. Should I use generics or trait objects? Write the handler trait and a registry that stores multiple handlers.
evals.expected_output: Should use trait objects (Box<dyn Handler>) for runtime polymorphism with heterogeneous collection.

assertions.text: Uses dyn Trait for runtime polymorphism
Uses Box<dyn Handler> or Vec<Box<dyn Handler>>
Explains why trait objects over generics
assertions.description: Should explain that runtime registration of different concrete types requires dynamic dispatch, not monomorphization

evals.name: collect-result-vec
evals.prompt: I'm parsing a list of strings into numbers. Write code that collects the results and stops at the first error using iterators.
evals.expected_output: Should use collect::<Result<Vec<T>, E>>() for fail-fast behavior.

assertions.text: Uses collect into Result<Vec<T>, E>
Does not collect into Vec<Result>
Uses iterator chain (map + collect)
